title: Cultural interactions between Europeans, Native Americans, and Africans
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on cultural interactions between Europeans, Native Americans, and Africans
topic_path: Cultural interactions between Europeans, Native Americans, and Africans | 1.6
Cultural interactions between Europeans, Native Americans, and Africans

assessment_items.question_content:
"The creation of the middle ground involved a process of mutual invention by both the French and the Algonquians. This process passed through various stages, of which the earliest is at once the most noticed and the least interesting. It was in this initial stage that the French, for example, simply assimilated Indians into their own conceptual order. Indians became *[[☃ definition 1]]*, and the French reduced Indian religion to devil worship and witchcraft. Algonquians, for their part, thought of the first Europeans as manitous [spirits]. On both sides, new people were crammed into existing categories in a mechanical way."

>-Source: Richard White, historian, *The Middle Ground,* 1991

**Present-day historians would most likely agree with which of the following aspects of the historical interpretation advanced in the passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: French word meaning wild or savage

choices.content: Europeans and indigenous people had diverging worldviews and struggled to understand each other.
French traders fully assimilated indigenous people into their culture through trade relationships.
French traders and Algonquians had a relationship of mutual respect and understanding.
Early interactions between Europeans and indigenous people were less significant than later ones.

assessment_items.question_content:
"The creation of the middle ground involved a process of mutual invention by both the French and the Algonquians. This process passed through various stages, of which the earliest is at once the most noticed and the least interesting. It was in this initial stage that the French, for example, simply assimilated Indians into their own conceptual order. Indians became *[[☃ definition 1]]*, and the French reduced Indian religion to devil worship and witchcraft. Algonquians, for their part, thought of the first Europeans as manitous [spirits]. On both sides, new people were crammed into existing categories in a mechanical way."

>-Source: Richard White, historian, *The Middle Ground,* 1991

**Which of the following cultural and social shifts resulted most directly from the trends described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: French word meaning wild or savage

choices.content: Europeans and indigenous people adopted useful aspects of each other’s culture.
Europeans adopted African slavery after disease diminished the indigenous population.
Indigenous people adopted a pan-Indian alliance to resist European invasion.
Europeans adopted Catholicism to convert enslaved Africans and indigenous people.

assessment_items.question_content:
"The creation of the middle ground involved a process of mutual invention by both the French and the Algonquians. This process passed through various stages, of which the earliest is at once the most noticed and the least interesting. It was in this initial stage that the French, for example, simply assimilated Indians into their own conceptual order. Indians became *[[☃ definition 1]]*, and the French reduced Indian religion to devil worship and witchcraft. Algonquians, for their part, thought of the first Europeans as manitous [spirits]. On both sides, new people were crammed into existing categories in a mechanical way."

>-Source: Richard White, historian, *The Middle Ground,* 1991

**Which of the following best supports the general argument in the excerpt about how Native Americans and Europeans related to each other in the early colonial period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: French word meaning wild or savage

choices.content: mutual misunderstandings about the terms of land ownership
indigenous peoples’ conversions to Catholicism at Spanish missions
competition among indigenous nations for European allies
indigenous nations’ military resistance to European encroachment

assessment_items.question_content:
>"They sent one canoe with three men, one of which, when they came near unto us, spoke in his language very loud and very boldly; seeming as though he would know why we were there, and by pointing with his oar towards the sea, we conjectured he meant we should be gone. But when we showed them knives and their use, by cutting of sticks and other trifles, they came close aboard our ship, as desirous to entertain our friendship. To these we gave such things as we perceived they liked." 

>-Source: George Waymouth, 1605

**The point of view expressed in the quotation above is most likely that of:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a European explorer encountering indigenous people.
an indigenous explorer encountering Europeans.
an African man encountering European slave traders.
an indigenous person encountering enslaved Africans.

assessment_items.question_content:
>"They sent one canoe with three men, one of which, when they came near unto us, spoke in his language very loud and very boldly; seeming as though he would know why we were there, and by pointing with his oar towards the sea, we conjectured he meant we should be gone. But when we showed them knives and their use, by cutting of sticks and other trifles, they came close aboard our ship, as desirous to entertain our friendship. To these we gave such things as we perceived they liked." 

>-Source: George Waymouth, 1605

**The exchange described in the excerpt is an example of which of the following developments in the early colonial period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how Europeans and indigenous peoples adopted useful aspects of each other’s culture
how indigenous groups migrated to coastal areas to trade with Europeans
how deadly epidemics devastated indigenous populations after contact with Europeans
how superior European military technology aided in the conquest of indigenous territory

assessment_items.question_content:
>"They sent one canoe with three men, one of which, when they came near unto us, spoke in his language very loud and very boldly; seeming as though he would know why we were there, and by pointing with his oar towards the sea, we conjectured he meant we should be gone. But when we showed them knives and their use, by cutting of sticks and other trifles, they came close aboard our ship, as desirous to entertain our friendship. To these we gave such things as we perceived they liked." 

>-Source: George Waymouth, 1605

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: trade relationships between Europeans and indigenous peoples
religious syncretism combining indigenous and Catholic beliefs
military conflict between Europeans and indigenous groups
race-based caste systems in colonial societies

assessment_items.question_content:
"When the [[☃ definition 1]] arrived at the pueblo of Acoma he asked the Indians for provisions for his trip and gave them in exchange hatchets and many other things. . . . and then the Indians very unwillingly gave some maize and tortillas. Being told that what the Spaniards needed most was flour, the Indians replied that they had none on hand, but that the Spaniards might leave and return for it the next day. . . . When the maese de campo went to the pueblo with his eighteen men to get the flour . . . the Indians, with treachery and premeditation, after inviting them to come up to their pueblo, killed the maese de campo, Captain Felipe de Escalante, Captain Diego Nuñez, eight soldiers, and two servants . . ."

>-Source: Juan de Oñate, Testimony in the Trial of the Indians of Acoma, 1598

**Which of the following most directly contributed to the conflict referred to in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: Spanish military rank, denoting the officer who was second in command to the colonial governor

choices.content: increasing European demands upon indigenous resources
declining values of European trade goods in American markets
increased Catholic missionary activity among indigenous communities
decreasing European supervision of colonial governments

assessment_items.question_content:
"When the [[☃ definition 1]] arrived at the pueblo of Acoma he asked the Indians for provisions for his trip and gave them in exchange hatchets and many other things. . . . and then the Indians very unwillingly gave some maize and tortillas. Being told that what the Spaniards needed most was flour, the Indians replied that they had none on hand, but that the Spaniards might leave and return for it the next day. . . . When the maese de campo went to the pueblo with his eighteen men to get the flour . . . the Indians, with treachery and premeditation, after inviting them to come up to their pueblo, killed the maese de campo, Captain Felipe de Escalante, Captain Diego Nuñez, eight soldiers, and two servants . . ."

>-Source: Juan de Oñate, Testimony in the Trial of the Indians of Acoma, 1598

**Juan de Oñate most likely gave this account for which of the following reasons?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: Spanish military rank, denoting the officer who was second in command to the colonial governor

choices.content: to persuade the Spanish courts to punish the Acoma nation
to explain the reasons why the Acoma people killed Spanish soldiers
to dispute false accounts of Spanish cruelty in colonial America
to praise the actions of Spanish soldiers at the Acoma Pueblo

assessment_items.question_content:
"When the [[☃ definition 1]] arrived at the pueblo of Acoma he asked the Indians for provisions for his trip and gave them in exchange hatchets and many other things. . . . and then the Indians very unwillingly gave some maize and tortillas. Being told that what the Spaniards needed most was flour, the Indians replied that they had none on hand, but that the Spaniards might leave and return for it the next day. . . . When the maese de campo went to the pueblo with his eighteen men to get the flour . . . the Indians, with treachery and premeditation, after inviting them to come up to their pueblo, killed the maese de campo, Captain Felipe de Escalante, Captain Diego Nuñez, eight soldiers, and two servants . . ."

>-Source: Juan de Oñate, Testimony in the Trial of the Indians of Acoma, 1598

**The excerpt most directly reflects which of the following trends?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: Spanish military rank, denoting the officer who was second in command to the colonial governor

choices.content: indigenous military resistance to European encroachment
shrinking Spanish influence in North America after 1500
European adoption of useful aspects of indigenous cultures
competition between European powers for natural resources

assessment_items.question_content:
>"It is written in the Book of Proverbs:  "He who is stupid will serve the wise man."  And so it is with the barbarous and inhumane peoples who have no civil life and peaceful customs.  It will always be just and in conformity with natural law that such people submit to the rule of more cultured and humane princes and nations.  Thanks to their virtues and the practical wisdom of their laws, the latter can destroy barbarism and educate these people to a more humane and virtuous life.  And if the latter reject such rule, it can be imposed upon them by force of arms."
	
>-Source: Juan Ginés de Sepúlveda, *On the Reasons for the Just War among the Indians,* 1547

**Which of the following groups would have been most likely to support Sepúlveda’s views as expressed in this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Spanish *encomenderos*
Puebloan peoples
West African slave traders
French fur traders

assessment_items.question_content:

It will always be just and in conformity with natural law that such people submit to the rule of more cultured and humane princes and nations.  Thanks to their virtues and the practical wisdom of their laws, the latter can destroy barbarism and educate these people to a more humane and virtuous life.  And if the latter reject such rule, it can be imposed upon them by force of arms."
	
>-Source: Juan Ginés de Sepúlveda, *On the Reasons for the Just War among the Indians,* 1547

**Which of the following most directly undermines Sepúlveda’s assertions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Spanish cruelty towards indigenous people in the Americas
indigenous military resistance to European invasion
European missionary activity among indigenous people
French trading relationships with fur trappers

assessment_items.question_content:

Thanks to their virtues and the practical wisdom of their laws, the latter can destroy barbarism and educate these people to a more humane and virtuous life.  And if the latter reject such rule, it can be imposed upon them by force of arms."
	
>-Source: Juan Ginés de Sepúlveda, *On the Reasons for the Just War among the Indians,* 1547

**The excerpt was most likely intended to increase public support for which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: European conquest in the Americas
efforts to combine indigenous beliefs with Christianity
new trade missions to India and China
institutions of higher education in Europe
